primary_speaker: Alex Stephens (VP Business Development, Digital Alloys)
topics: wire feedstock vs. powder feedstock economics
thermal management and residual stresses in 3D printing
isotropy vs anisotropy in printed parts
notes: Guest lecture by Alex Stephens of Digital Alloys. Tom Eagar appears in moderator and interjector role only; he introduces the speaker, asks clarifying questions, and adds metallurgical context at key points (p21 chemical milling, p25 alloy specifics, p32 thermal management history, p36 widmanstätten microstructure, p51 die economics, p58 binder jet). The audio quality on the captioner side appears to be poor for Tom's interjections (multiple unresolvable garblings); Alex's presenter audio is much cleaner. Section breaks for Layer 3 should track Alex's teaching units: (s1) self-introduction and overview of metal AM taxonomy; (s2) business value drivers — prototyping, tooling, machined-part replacement; (s3) thermal management, microstructure, materials limitations; (s4) Joule Printing process described; (s5) toolpath and isotropy/multi-metal future; (s6) target applications — aerospace titanium, automotive tool steel; (s7) hybrid machines and process selection.
